w:r.w:t: Longitudinal Clinical Reasoning with Memory-Augmented Generation
Solo Academic Course Project • 14-Week Plan • Healthcare AI Domain

GraphMed is a long-term memory-augmented clinical assistant that maintains, evolves, and reasons over a patient's knowledge graph across multiple hospital visits. Unlike standard RAG-based systems that treat every interaction as stateless, GraphMed builds a per-patient, temporally-aware knowledge graph that grows with every visit — detecting contradictions, tracking lab trends, and grounding all clinical answers in verified sources.

Current LLM-based clinical tools forget everything between sessions. A doctor reading a patient file remembers that blood pressure has been rising for 6 months; a standard LLM does not. This project solves that by giving the LLM a living, evolving medical memory structured as a knowledge graph — enabling longitudinal, grounded, and conflict-aware clinical reasoning.
No existing system combines temporal graph evolution + long-term vector memory + GraphRAG + agentic conflict resolution in a single clinical pipeline.
The knowledge graph evolves with every visit — old facts decay, new ones are added, contradictions are flagged automatically.
A LoRA fine-tuned conflict classifier detects clinical contradictions (e.g., allergy stated then denied) faster and more accurately than prompting the full LLM.
The ReAct agent reasons step-by-step, uses tools, and updates memory after every interaction — making the system auditable and explainable.
GraphMed gives an LLM a living, evolving medical memory — so it reasons like a doctor who actually remembers your history, not one who meets you for the first time every visit."

MIMIC-III is partially sufficient. It is ICU-focused and contains discharge summaries, lab results, medication records, and diagnostic codes spanning over a decade of care — making it excellent for clinical language and lab trend modelling. However, for longitudinal outpatient scenarios, it is not ideal alone. Use the following three-tier data strategy:
Fallback if access is delayed; valid for course projects
Apply for PhysioNet access immediately — it takes 2–5 business days. While waiting, start building the pipeline with 10–20 synthetic patient records.

Yes — specifically GraphRAG, not plain RAG. Standard RAG retrieves flat text chunks. GraphRAG traverses entity relationships across the knowledge graph before retrieval, enabling multi-hop reasoning. For example: 'Is Ibuprofen safe?' triggers a graph traversal: Patient → Metformin → contraindicated_with → NSAIDs → Ibuprofen. The RAG then fetches supporting clinical literature to ground the answer.
2.3 LLM APIs — Free Alternatives (No OpenAI Required)
All LLM inference in this project uses free or very-low-cost APIs. OpenAI is not required.
groq.com → free tier, no credit card needed
huggingface.co → free API token in settings
ollama.com → install locally, no internet needed
together.ai → sign up for free credits
Use Groq API for agent reasoning (fast, free, LLaMA 3), HuggingFace Inference API for entity extraction (BioMedLM), and Ollama locally as a backup. Total cost: ₹0.

The project title 'Temporally-Evolving Patient Knowledge Graphs for Longitudinal Clinical Reasoning' is your original contribution title. You build on three real published base papers as foundational prior work:
LLM-DA, NeurIPS 2024: Dynamic adaptation for temporal KG reasoning using LLMs to extract and update temporal logical rules
Temporal graph evolution logic and confidence decay mechanism
GraphRAG-based sepsis clinical decision support using GPT-4 and Neo4j (2024)
GraphRAG architecture for clinical multi-hop reasoning
DR.KNOWS: Integrating UMLS knowledge graphs with LLMs for case-specific diagnosis prediction from EHR data
Patient-specific KG construction from clinical notes
VS Code — install Python, Jupyter, and GitLens extensions
Google Colab (free GPU for LoRA fine-tuning) — use Colab Pro if available
GitHub — create a private repo from day one
pip with requirements.txt — pin all versions
# Install all dependencies in one command
pip install langchain langgraph chromadb networkx pyvis
pip install transformers peft datasets accelerate bitsandbytes
pip install pandas numpy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn
pip install streamlit evaluate bert-score rouge-score
Get all tools installed, accounts created, and a working skeleton project structure in GitHub before writing a single line of project code.
Create a GitHub repo named graphmed — add README.md, .gitignore (Python), and a /src folder structure.
Set up a Python virtual environment: python -m venv graphmed-env and activate it.
Install all dependencies from Section 3.2 above. Pin versions in requirements.txt immediately.
Create accounts on: PhysioNet (apply for MIMIC access), Groq (free API key), HuggingFace (free token), Weights & Biases (free experiment tracker).
Install Ollama locally as a free offline fallback. Run: ollama pull mistral to download Mistral 7B.
Test your Groq API key with a simple hello-world call to confirm the pipeline is working.
# Test Groq API (paste your key from groq.com)
Working repo, all accounts active, Groq API test passing. Estimated time: 1 full day.
Prepare a clean, structured dataset of patient timelines with at least 3 visits per patient. If MIMIC access is pending, build a synthetic dataset to start immediately.

After PhysioNet approval, download the following files: NOTEEVENTS.csv, LABEVENTS.csv, PRESCRIPTIONS.csv, DIAGNOSES_ICD.csv, ADMISSIONS.csv. Filter for patients with 3 or more admissions using the SUBJECT_ID and HADM_ID columns.
# Filter all tables to these patients
Option B — Synthetic data (use while waiting for MIMIC access)
Use this Groq prompt to generate 30 synthetic patient timelines. This is academically valid for a course project and will let you start building immediately.
Generate a realistic synthetic patient medical timeline.
Patient has Type 2 Diabetes and Hypertension.
Each visit must include: date, clinical note (3–5 sentences),
lab values (HbA1c, BP, eGFR), medications, and symptoms.
Return ONLY valid JSON. No extra text.
Format: {patient_id, visits: [{date, note, labs, medications, symptoms}]}
50–100 patient timelines (real or synthetic) saved as JSON files, one per patient. Estimated time: 4–5 days.
Build a pipeline that takes a raw clinical note and outputs structured entities: conditions, medications, lab values, symptoms, and procedures. This is the input layer for the knowledge graph.
Fast and free — runs entirely locally
Good at recognising medical terms, drug names, and conditions
Use model: en_core_sci_md for general biomedical text
Stage 2 — LLM-based structured extraction using Groq
Catches relationships and context that NER misses
Converts raw text to structured JSON
Use for: relation extraction, temporal context, severity
prompt = f'''Extract medical entities from the clinical note.
Return ONLY valid JSON with keys:
temperature=0.1 # Low temp for structured output
extract_entities() function working on 20+ test notes with >80% accuracy on manually verified spot checks. Estimated time: 3 days.

Build a per-patient, temporally-aware knowledge graph using NetworkX. Each node represents a medical entity (condition, drug, lab value, symptom). Each edge represents a clinical relationship (managed_by, caused_by, contraindicated_with, measured_by). Every node stores a timestamp and confidence score.
def add_entity(self, name: str, entity_type: str, date: str, confidence=1.0):
def add_relation(self, src: str, dst: str, relation: str, date: str):
# Nodes not confirmed in recent visits lose confidence
Building the graph from visit data
# Add medication nodes and link to conditions
# Link to conditions (LLM infers which drug treats which condition)
# Add lab value nodes with trend tracking
for src, dst, data in pkg.G.edges(data=True):
PatientKnowledgeGraph class working for 10 patients, each with 3+ visits. Graph visualised and verified manually. Estimated time: 4 days.

Store full visit summaries as semantic embeddings in ChromaDB — capturing contextual nuance (patient emotions, doctor notes, ambiguous language) that the structured graph cannot. The graph holds facts; the memory store holds the story.
embedder = SentenceTransformer('all-MiniLM-L6-v2') # Free, runs on CPU
def retrieve_relevant_memory(patient_id: str, query: str, top_k=3) -> list:
All patient visits indexed in ChromaDB. retrieve_relevant_memory() returning semantically relevant past visits for test queries. Estimated time: 2 days.

Build a separate medical knowledge base (not patient-specific) containing drug interactions, clinical guidelines, and disease information. This is the external reference that grounds the agent's answers in verified medical facts.
Free data sources for the medical KB
Drug names, interactions, mechanisms — download free XML from drugbank.com/releases
Free REST API: eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov — search by condition or drug name
Patient-friendly drug info — scrape or download via MedlinePlus Connect API
Comprehensive medical concepts — free with NLM license (apply at uts.nlm.nih.gov)
Treatment guidelines as PDF — parse with pdfplumber and chunk into KB
# Build medical KB from DrugBank CSV export
Medical KB indexed with 500+ drug entries and 200+ clinical facts. verify_fact() returning grounded answers with source attribution. Estimated time: 3 days.
Build the brain of GraphMed — a ReAct-style agent that decides which tools to use, reasons step-by-step, and produces grounded clinical answers. Use LangGraph for stateful, multi-turn agent flows.
def query_graph(patient_id: str, query_type: str) -> str:
'''Fetch facts from the patient knowledge graph. query_type: medications, conditions, labs, trends'''
nodes = [n for n,d in pkg.G.nodes(data=True) if d.get('type')==query_type.upper()]
def retrieve_memory(patient_id: str, topic: str) -> str:
'''Retrieve relevant past visit context from vector memory.'''
'''Look up medical facts, drug interactions, guidelines in the knowledge base.'''
def detect_conflict(statement_a: str, statement_b: str) -> str:
'''Check if two clinical statements contradict each other using the LoRA classifier.'''
def update_graph(patient_id: str, new_visit_json: str) -> str:
'''Add new visit data to the patient knowledge graph and evolve it.'''
SYSTEM_PROMPT = '''You are GraphMed, a clinical reasoning assistant.
You have access to a patient knowledge graph and long-term memory.
1. ALWAYS call query_graph before answering questions about the patient.
2. ALWAYS call verify_fact before stating any drug interaction.
3. ALWAYS call detect_conflict when two clinical facts seem to contradict.
4. ALWAYS call update_graph after a visit to evolve the memory.
5. Never state a fact you have not retrieved from a tool.
6. Cite which tool provided each piece of information.
'messages': [('user', 'Patient P001: Is it safe to prescribe Ibuprofen?')]
Agent correctly reasons through 20 test clinical queries, calls appropriate tools, and cites sources. Reasoning traces logged and reviewed. Estimated time: 5 days.

Fine-tune a small BERT-based model using LoRA to classify pairs of clinical statements as CONFLICT or CONSISTENT. This is faster and more accurate than asking the full LLM every time, and it demonstrates model adaptation — a key requirement of your project brief.
Use microsoft/BiomedNLP-BiomedBERT-base-uncased from HuggingFace. It is pre-trained on biomedical literature and free to use. Fine-tuning on Colab free tier takes approximately 20 minutes.
# TIP: Use Groq to auto-generate 200 pairs from your patient data:
# Prompt: 'Generate 20 CONFLICT pairs and 20 CONSISTENT pairs of
# clinical statements about diabetes and hypertension patients.'
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification, TrainingArguments, Trainer
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
# LoRA config — adds only ~0.5% new parameters
r=8, # Rank — keep low for fast training
# Training args — tuned for Colab free tier
LoRA model trained with >85% F1 on conflict detection. detect_conflict() tool integrated into the ReAct agent. Estimated time: 4 days.

Implement the three core graph evolution operations: Addition (new facts added), Update (existing facts revised with new values), and Conflict Resolution (contradictions detected and flagged or auto-resolved).
for node_name, node_data in extract_new_nodes(entities, date).items():
# OPERATION 1: ADDITION — new entity not previously seen
# OPERATION 2: UPDATE — entity exists, value changed (e.g. lab result)
# OPERATION 3: CONFLICT — existing node contradicts new fact
Graph evolution working correctly on a simulated 4-visit patient timeline. All three operations (add, update, conflict) verified with print logs. Estimated time: 3 days.
Run three experiments comparing GraphMed against a baseline static RAG system. This is the core academic contribution — your results table.

Build a simple baseline: same LLM (LLaMA 3.1 8B via Groq) with standard RAG (ChromaDB flat retrieval) but no knowledge graph, no memory evolution, and no conflict detection. This isolates the contribution of each GraphMed component.
Experiment 1 — Factual accuracy on clinical QA
50 clinical questions about patient history, drug safety, and lab trends
Run both Baseline RAG and GraphMed on all 50 questions
FactScore (automated) + manual binary correct/incorrect on 20 sampled answers
GraphMed should outperform on questions requiring multi-visit context
Ask the same factual question about Visit 1 after Visit 4 has been processed
BERTScore between expected answer and actual answer; custom consistency score
Baseline RAG forgets visit 1 facts; GraphMed retains them via the graph
Inject 30 known contradictions into patient timelines and test detection rate
20 drug conflicts + 10 allergy/diagnosis contradictions injected manually
Precision, Recall, F1 on conflict detection vs. no-classifier baseline
LoRA classifier achieves >85% F1; pure LLM prompt achieves ~60% F1
Remove one component at a time and measure performance drop. This proves each component contributes independently:
Knowledge graph removed, vector memory only
Conflict classifier replaced with LLM prompt
Results table with all three experiments and ablation study. At minimum 2 of the 3 experiments must show statistically meaningful improvement over baseline. Estimated time: 5 days.

Build an interactive demo app that showcases GraphMed's capabilities. The demo must show: patient selection, knowledge graph visualisation, a chat interface with visible reasoning traces, and graph evolution across visits.
# app.py — run with: streamlit run app.py
Working Streamlit demo deployed locally. Screen recording made for submission. Estimated time: 3 days.
Problem, approach, key results — write last
Clinical AI gap, why memory matters, paper contributions
7 sections from literature review outline — 2–3 pages
Each phase with key design choices and code snippets
Three experiments with tables and graphs
Four system variants compared — prove each component adds value
What worked, what did not, limitations, threats to validity
Summary + future work directions (federated learning, real-time EHR)
30–40 citations using IEEE or ACM format
Code on GitHub (clean, commented, README with setup instructions) | Report as PDF | Streamlit demo screen recording | Results tables reproduced | All citations verified
Start immediately with 30 synthetic patients. Apply on day 1.
Switch to Ollama local (Mistral 7B). Already installed in Phase 0.
LoRA fine-tuning fails on Colab free tier
Use smaller BiomedBERT-tiny variant. Reduce batch size to 8.
Graph gets too large to query efficiently
Limit to 50 patients × 5 visits. Add node pruning for confidence < 0.2.
Supplement with pure LLM extraction via Groq. Already planned in Phase 2.
LangGraph agent goes into reasoning loops
Set max_iterations=10. Add a fallback direct-answer mode.
Keep this page visible while building. These are the commands and keys you will use most.
# Fine-tune on Colab — upload your script and run
console.groq.com — sign up, go to API Keys
api.together.ai — sign up, no credit card needed
wandb.ai — free tier, get key from settings
physionet.org — register, complete CITI training, request access
│ ├── medical_kb.py # Phase 5: Medical RAG knowledge base
This document is your complete reference for building GraphMed end-to-end. Work through each phase in order — each one builds directly on the last. Good luck.
